questions.question: Explain the two-level directory structure and highlight its advantages over a single-level directory.
keyPoints: Single-level: all files in one directory causing name collisions.
Two-level: separate directory for each user, preventing name clashes.
User directories map to a master file directory (MFD) and user file directories (UFD).
Simplifies file management and improves security by isolating users.

questions.question: Describe the hierarchical (tree-structured) directory organization. How are absolute and relative path conventions used to locate files?
keyPoints: Root directory branches into subdirectories forming a tree.
Relative path is from the current working directory (e.g., ../user/file.txt).
Supports nested folders, easy navigation, and logical organization.

questions.question: Discuss the network (graph) directory structure. Compare it with a hierarchical tree directory in terms of file sharing, naming, and traversal. Include a practical example of implementing network directories using symbolic links and mention issues such as cycles.
keyPoints: Network directory allows files/directories to have multiple parents (multiple references).
Supports sharing and cross-filesystem links but introduces potential cycles.
Symbolic (soft) links create references; hard links are limited to same partition.
Cycle handling requires detection (e.g., reference counting) to avoid infinite loops.

questions.question: Explain the main methods for implementing directory structures: linear list, hash table, and B+ tree. Analyse the time complexity of basic directory operations (create, delete, search, rename) and discuss which method is suitable for large-scale file systems.
keyPoints: Linear list: simple O(n) search, O(1) append, high insertion cost.
Hash table: average O(1) search/insert, collision handling needed.
B+ tree: O(log n) search, balanced, efficient for range queries.
Large systems prefer B+ trees or hash tables for scalability.
